text:
Healthcare systems globally are significant producers of greenhouse gas emissions, with medical imaging identified as a major contributor within the clinical environment. As awareness of man-made climate change increases, there is an urgent need to decarbonize radiology through evidence-based, sustainable practices. This review explores the primary sources of emissions in imaging departments, ranging from the high energy consumption of MRI and CT scanners—even when idle—to the environmental costs of data storage and single-use plastics in interventional procedures. 

The paper outlines practical mitigation strategies, such as optimizing patient scheduling, implementing green "shut-down" protocols for reporting stations, and prioritizing lower-energy modalities when clinically appropriate. It also introduces the concepts of the circular economy and "digital temperance" as frameworks for long-term reduction in waste and resource consumption. By addressing both direct operations and Scope 3 supply chain emissions, radiologists and imaging staff can play a pivotal role in transitioning toward a greener healthcare model. This article provides actionable insights to empower radiology departments to lower their carbon footprint without compromising patient care.
